core_behavior: The system must authenticate a user by verifying their credentials against a stored identity record and, upon success, establish an authenticated session or issue an authentication token.
The login flow must complete end-to-end (from credential submission to session/token issuance) within defined performance thresholds.
Accept a username/email and password from the user.
Locate the user record by identifier.
Compare the submitted password against the stored credential hash.
On success, produce a session token (JWT or equivalent) and return it to the client.
On failure, return a structured error and apply rate limiting.
Invalidate the session or token on demand (Logout).
inputs: `identifier`: string, required; Email address or username; max 254 characters; case-insensitive.
`password`: string, required; 8–128 characters; transmitted only over TLS.
`client_metadata`: object, optional; IP address, User-Agent string, device fingerprint (collected server-side or from headers).
`mfa_code`: string, conditional; 6-digit TOTP or 8-digit backup code; required if MFA is enabled on the account.
`remember_me`: boolean, optional; Defaults to `false`; extends session/token lifetime if `true`.
All inputs must be received via an HTTPS POST request to `/auth/login` with a `Content-Type: application/json` body.
outputs: Success response HTTP 200 with `status`, `access_token` (JWT), `refresh_token` (opaque token), `token_type` (Bearer), `expires_in` (900), and `user` object containing `id`, `email`, `display_name`, and `roles`.
`access_token`: Short-lived JWT (15 minutes default; 30 minutes if `remember_me = true`).
`refresh_token`: Long-lived opaque token (7 days default; 30 days if `remember_me = true`), stored as an `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite=Strict` cookie in addition to the response body.
MFA Challenge response HTTP 202 with `status` (`mfa_required`), `mfa_token` (short-lived opaque token), and `expires_in` (300).
assumptions: JWT is used for access tokens (stateless verification by downstream services); refresh tokens are opaque and stored server-side in a fast key-value store (e.g., Redis) to allow revocation.
Passwords are stored as Argon2id hashes (memory: 64 MB, iterations: 3, parallelism: 4). bcrypt (cost 12) is an acceptable fallback for legacy records.
Email addresses are lowercased and Unicode-normalized (NFC) before lookup and storage. Usernames are lowercased only.
TOTP (RFC 6238) with a 30-second window and ±1 step tolerance.
failure_modes: On failure, return a structured error and apply rate limiting.
Error code `INVALID_CREDENTIALS` returned on credential mismatch.
Error code `ACCOUNT_LOCKED` returned when account is locked.
Error code `ACCOUNT_DISABLED` returned when account is disabled.
Error code `MFA_REQUIRED` returned when MFA is required but not provided.
Error code `MFA_INVALID` returned when MFA code is invalid.
Error code `RATE_LIMITED` returned when rate limit is exceeded.
Error code `VALIDATION_ERROR` returned on input validation failure.
edge_cases: `mfa_code` is required if MFA is enabled on the account; accepts either a 6-digit TOTP or 8-digit backup code.
`remember_me = true` extends access token lifetime to 30 minutes and refresh token lifetime to 30 days.
bcrypt (cost 12) is an acceptable fallback for legacy records when Argon2id is not used.
security_constraints: `password` must be transmitted only over TLS.
All inputs must be received via an HTTPS POST request to `/auth/login`.
Rate limiting applied on login failure.
Refresh tokens are stored server-side to allow revocation.
Passwords are stored as Argon2id hashes (memory: 64 MB, iterations: 3, parallelism: 4).
Email addresses are lowercased and Unicode-normalized (NFC) before lookup and storage.
performance_constraints: The login flow must complete end-to-end (from credential submission to session/token issuance) within defined performance thresholds.
Refresh tokens are stored server-side in a fast key-value store (e.g., Redis).
Access token expires in 900 seconds by default.
